# Abstraction Leakage

## The Physics

Every abstraction leaks. A database query planner hides storage layout — until
a query takes thirty seconds instead of thirty milliseconds and the developer
must understand index structure. A network protocol hides packet routing —
until latency spikes and the operator must reason about physical topology. A
programming language hides machine instructions — until a performance-critical
loop requires understanding of cache lines and branch prediction.

This is not a defect in any particular abstraction. It is a fundamental
property of abstraction itself. An abstraction works by discarding information
— that is what makes it useful. But the discarded information does not
disappear. It remains present in the implementation, and under certain
conditions it forces its way back into the consumer's awareness.

The engineering question is therefore never "does this abstraction leak?" — it
always does. The question is: which implementation details escape, under what
conditions, and what damage do they cause when they do? Well-designed
abstractions leak predictably at well-documented seams. Poorly-designed ones
leak unpredictably, forcing consumers into reverse-engineering exercises during
production incidents.

## Why This Matters

- **Boundary design**: Explicit boundaries must document their known leak points, not pretend they don't exist.
- **Dependency management**: Consumers who depend on leaked implementation details create invisible coupling.
- **Debugging**: The most time-consuming production issues frequently occur at abstraction boundaries where leaked details interact.
- **Staffing**: Teams operating above an abstraction still need access to expertise below it, because leaks are certain.

## Observable Patterns

- Performance problems disproportionately cluster at abstraction boundaries.
- "It works on my machine" is almost always an abstraction leak — the local environment abstracts away differences that production exposes.
- ORMs that hide SQL work until they don't, producing N+1 queries that force developers back to the SQL layer.
- Cloud provider abstractions leak region-specific behavior, availability zone topology, and rate limits.

## 1970/2070 Note

A 1970 COBOL programmer relied on sequential file abstractions that leaked
disk seek latency. A 2070 quantum systems engineer will rely on logical qubit
abstractions that leak physical error rates. The promise of abstraction is
always partial. The leakage is always real.

## Relationship to Meta Layer

- JOS-P14 (Explicit Boundaries) — boundaries are the seams where leakage is managed, not eliminated
- JOS-P20 (Acyclic Dependencies) — leaked details that create untracked dependencies introduce hidden cycles
